insights: All 500 IT service tickets contain identical short_description text ('There was an issue'), eliminating the possibility of identifying 'repeated identical expense claims' based on claim narrative or justification.

Only 4 unique caller_id values exist across the dataset, severely limiting user-level behavioral analysis and making it statistically impossible to distinguish fraudulent repetition from normal variation in a small, non-representative user cohort.

Every caller-category combination (20 total) shows 19–33 submissions over a 13-month period, with average intervals of 11–21 days and high dispersion—patterns fully consistent with routine, non-malicious IT ticket submission behavior.

Isolated very-short intervals (1–3 hours) between same-caller/same-category submissions occur infrequently and lack systematic clustering, indicating operational urgency or timing coincidence rather than coordinated duplicate submission.
No exact duplicate records exist—each ticket has a unique opened_at timestamp and unique number—ruling out technical duplication or data ingestion errors as a source of apparent repetition.

The dataset fundamentally lacks essential attributes required for expense claim fraud detection: no monetary amounts, no receipt identifiers, no expense category codes, no policy violation flags, and no claim justification variability.
summary: The analysis conclusively finds no evidence of fraudulent duplicate expense claims, because the provided dataset consists entirely of homogeneous IT service tickets—not expense claims—and lacks all necessary fraud-detection attributes (e.g., amounts, receipts, descriptive justifications). With only 4 callers and zero variation in claim description, observed repetition patterns are structurally inevitable and operationally benign, reflecting routine IT support demand rather than policy abuse. The uniformity of short_description invalidates the core premise of detecting 'identical claims' by content, and the absence of financial or policy-related fields renders fraud assessment infeasible. To investigate expense claim fraud, a new dataset must include per-claim monetary values, itemized descriptions, receipt hashes or IDs, submission timestamps with millisecond precision, and explicit expense category mappings aligned to organizational policy rules.
